1:
"For as touching the ministering to the saints, it is superfluous for me to write to you:" The brief verse notes Paul's saying it is needless to write further of the collection. The grammatical voices note the turn. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, continuing the subject of the collection ("the ministering to the saints" — the relief of the poor believers at Jerusalem), says it is "superfluous (needless)" to write further — they already know their duty and are disposed to it.

On the rhetorical touch, the witnesses note the delicacy. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul's tact: by saying it is needless to urge them, he gently commends their willingness while still pressing the matter; a graceful way of encouraging without seeming to doubt them.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the art: Paul flatters their good will even as he stirs it; the disclaimer is itself a spur. The verse displays Paul's saying it is needless to write further of the collection.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, continuing the subject of the collection ("the ministering to the saints"), says it is "superfluous (needless)" to write further, since they already know their duty (Barnes, Robertson); his tact in commending their willingness while still pressing the matter, encouraging without seeming to doubt (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Henry note the disclaimer is itself a spur. The verse displays the needlessness of writing. The verse marks the subject: ministering to the saints. The verse displays the disclaimer: superfluous to write. The verse displays the tact: commending their will. The verse displays the spur: gentle. The disclaimer displays Paul encouraging by professing confidence. The verse displays the ministering: to the saints. The verse displays the writing: needless. The verse displays the delicacy: of the appeal. Paul's saying it is needless to write further of the collection displays his pastoral tact: by professing that he need not urge so willing a church, he gently commends their good disposition even as he keeps the matter before them. The verse displays the graceful opening of the appeal, grounded in their known readiness (v.2).
2:
"For I know the forwardness of your mind, for which I boast of you to them of Macedonia, that Achaia was ready a year ago; and your zeal hath provoked very many." The brief verse notes Paul's boasting of the Corinthians' readiness to the Macedonians. The grammatical voices note the confidence. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "knows the forwardness (eagerness, readiness) of your mind" — he is sure of their willing disposition — and on the strength of it had "boasted of you to them of Macedonia, that Achaia (the province of which Corinth was chief) was ready a year ago."

On "your zeal hath provoked very many," the witnesses note the effect. Barnes and Calvin note that the Corinthians' "zeal" (in being early and eager) had "provoked (stirred up, stimulated) very many" — their example had moved many of the Macedonians to give; zeal is contagious.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the mutual spur: Paul had used the Corinthians' eagerness to stir the Macedonians, and now uses the Macedonians' example (ch. 8) to stir the Corinthians — each church provoking the other to good. The verse displays Paul's boasting of the Corinthians' readiness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "knows the forwardness of your mind" and on its strength had "boasted of you to the Macedonians, that Achaia was ready a year ago" (Barnes, Robertson); the Corinthians' "zeal" had "provoked very many" of the Macedonians to give, zeal being contagious (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the mutual spur between the churches. The verse displays Paul's boasting of their readiness. The verse marks the forwardness: their eagerness. The verse displays the boast: to Macedonia. The verse displays the effect: provoking many. The verse displays the contagion: of zeal. The boasting displays the Corinthians' example stirring the Macedonians. The verse displays the readiness: of Achaia. The verse displays the zeal: provoking. The verse displays the boast: vindicated by their giving. Paul's boasting of the Corinthians' readiness to the Macedonians displays the mutual stimulation of zeal: confident of their eager disposition, Paul had boasted that Achaia was ready a year ago, and their zeal had stirred up many — each church provoking the other to liberality. The verse displays the Corinthians' provoking example, which Paul would not have put to shame (vv.3-5).
3:
"Yet have I sent the brethren, lest our boasting of you should be in vain in this behalf; that, as I said, ye may be ready:" The brief verse notes the brethren sent so the Corinthians' giving would be ready. The grammatical voices note the precaution. Barnes and Robertson note that, despite his confidence, Paul "sent the brethren" (Titus and the two companions of ch. 8) "lest our boasting of you should be in vain (proved empty) in this behalf" — to ensure his boast was not put to shame.

On "that ye may be ready," the witnesses note the aim. Barnes and Calvin note the purpose: that the Corinthians might be fully "ready" with their gift when the time came; the brethren were sent to see the collection completed beforehand.

The Reformed Calvin notes the wisdom: Paul does not presume on their good will but takes practical means to secure the result; confidence and prudent action go together. The verse displays the brethren sent so the giving would be ready.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: despite his confidence, Paul "sent the brethren lest our boasting of you should be in vain in this behalf," to ensure his boast was not shamed (Barnes, Robertson); the aim that the Corinthians "may be ready" with their gift, the brethren sent to complete the collection beforehand (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul does not presume but takes practical means. The verse displays the brethren sent. The verse marks the precaution: lest the boast fail. The verse displays the aim: that ye be ready. The verse displays the means: the brethren sent. The verse displays the wisdom: confidence with action. The sending displays Paul securing the result by prudent means. The verse displays the boast: not to be vain. The verse displays the readiness: secured. The verse displays the prudence: of Paul. The brethren sent so the Corinthians' giving would be ready display Paul's prudent care: not presuming on his confidence, he sent the brethren ahead to see the collection completed, that his boast of them be not put to shame and they be fully ready. The verse displays the practical securing of the gift, lest both be ashamed (v.4).
4:
"Lest haply if they of Macedonia come with me, and find you unprepared, we (that we say not, ye) should be ashamed in this same confident boasting." The brief verse notes Paul's concern to avoid shame if the Corinthians were found unprepared. The grammatical voices note the danger. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's specific fear: that "if they of Macedonia come with me, and find you unprepared (the collection not ready)," both he and they would be put to shame "in this same confident boasting."

On "we (that we say not, ye)," the witnesses note the delicacy. Barnes and Ellicott note Paul's tact: he says "we should be ashamed" — placing the shame first on himself — and only by gentle implication adds "that we say not, ye," sparing them the direct charge while making the point.

The Reformed Calvin notes the motive: the honour of the Corinthians, of Paul, and of the cause were all at stake; Paul's concern was that all be spared the embarrassment of an empty boast. The verse displays Paul's concern to avoid shame if they were found unprepared.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's fear that "if they of Macedonia come with me, and find you unprepared," both he and they would be shamed "in this same confident boasting" (Barnes, Robertson); his tact in saying "we should be ashamed," placing the shame first on himself and only by implication adding "that we say not, ye," sparing them the direct charge (Barnes, Ellicott). Calvin notes the honour of all was at stake. The verse displays avoiding shame. The verse marks the danger: found unprepared. The verse displays the shame: of an empty boast. The verse displays the tact: we, not ye. The verse displays the motive: sparing all. The concern displays Paul guarding against the embarrassment of an empty boast. The verse displays the Macedonians: coming with him. The verse displays the unpreparedness: feared. The verse displays the delicacy: sparing them. Paul's concern to avoid shame if the Corinthians were found unprepared displays both his prudence and his tact: he feared that the Macedonians, finding the gift not ready, would put his confident boast to shame — and he gently lays that shame first on himself, sparing them the direct charge. The verse displays the apostle's delicate care, prompting the brethren's advance visit (v.5).
5:
"Therefore I thought it necessary to exhort the brethren, that they would go before unto you, and make up beforehand your bounty, whereof ye had notice before, that the same might be ready, as a matter of bounty, and not as of covetousness." The brief verse notes the brethren sent ahead to prepare the gift as a willing bounty. The grammatical voices note the arrangement. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "thought it necessary to exhort the brethren" to "go before (ahead of him)" to Corinth and "make up beforehand your bounty (the generous gift)" — to have the collection gathered and ready before his arrival.

On "as a matter of bounty, and not as of covetousness," the witnesses note the manner. Barnes and Calvin note the key distinction: the gift should be "as a matter of bounty (a generous, free offering)" and "not as of covetousness (not grudgingly, as something extorted)" — given freely, not squeezed out as if reluctantly.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the spirit: by gathering it beforehand and at leisure, the gift would be a true freewill offering, not a hurried, reluctant collection. The verse displays the brethren sent ahead to prepare the gift as a willing bounty.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "thought it necessary to exhort the brethren" to "go before" to Corinth and "make up beforehand your bounty," having the collection ready before his arrival (Barnes, Robertson); the gift to be "as a matter of bounty (a free offering), not as of covetousness (not grudgingly extorted)," given freely (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes that gathering it beforehand made it a true freewill offering. The verse displays preparing the gift as bounty. The verse marks the arrangement: brethren go before. The verse displays the preparation: made up beforehand. The verse displays the manner: bounty, not covetousness. The verse displays the spirit: freely given. The arrangement displays the gift prepared as a willing offering. The verse displays the going before: of the brethren. The verse displays the bounty: prepared. The verse displays the freeness: not grudging. The brethren sent ahead to prepare the gift as a willing bounty display Paul's care for the spirit of the giving: by having the collection gathered beforehand and at leisure, it would be a true, generous freewill offering and not a grudging, extorted sum. The verse displays the gift readied as bounty, grounded in the law of the harvest (v.6).
6:
"But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully." The witnesses note the law of the harvest applied to giving. On "he which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lays down a general law (drawn from agriculture): as in sowing, so in giving — "he which soweth sparingly (scantily, grudgingly) shall reap also sparingly"; a stingy giver reaps a scanty return.

On "he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully," the witnesses note the promise. Barnes and Henry note the reverse: "he which soweth bountifully (liberally, with blessings) shall reap also bountifully" — the generous giver reaps an abundant harvest; God rewards liberality liberally.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the comfort: the reaping is both spiritual and (as God sees fit) temporal; giving is a sowing that God repays, so liberality is never loss. The verse displays the law of the harvest applied to giving.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul lays down a general law from agriculture — "he which soweth sparingly (scantily, grudgingly) shall reap also sparingly," the stingy giver reaping a scanty return (Barnes, Robertson); and "he which soweth bountifully (liberally) shall reap also bountifully," the generous giver reaping an abundant harvest, God rewarding liberality liberally (Barnes, Henry). Calvin and Wesley note the reaping is spiritual and temporal; giving is a sowing God repays. The verse displays the law of the harvest. The verse marks the sparing: scant reaping. The verse displays the bountiful: abundant reaping. The verse displays the law: as sowing, so reaping. The verse displays the comfort: giving never loss. The law displays giving as a sowing God rewards. The verse displays the sowing: sparing or bountiful. The verse displays the reaping: in kind. The verse displays the harvest: of liberality. The law of the harvest applied to giving displays the great encouragement to liberality: as a man sows, so he reaps — the sparing giver a scanty return, the bountiful giver an abundant one — for giving is a sowing that God surely repays, and liberality is never loss. The verse displays the principle of bountiful sowing, grounded in the cheerful heart (v.7).
7:
"Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver." The witnesses note that giving should be willing and cheerful, not grudging. On "as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give," the grammatical voices note the deliberate resolve. Barnes and Ellicott note giving should follow a "purpose of the heart" — a deliberate, settled resolve (not a spent emotion nor a half-regretted promise) — each giving from a willing heart, the heart being more concerned in giving than the head.

On "not grudgingly, or of necessity," the witnesses note the two faults. Barnes and Calvin note the gift must not be "grudgingly (of grief, as if sorry to part with it)" nor "of necessity (by constraint, as if forced)" — both reluctance and compulsion spoil the offering; Clarke notes the Jewish distinction between the grudging legal alms and the cheerful freewill offering.

On "God loveth a cheerful giver," the witnesses note the great motive. Barnes and Calvin note the proverb (echoing the Greek of Proverbs 22:8): God "loveth a cheerful (hilaron, hilarious, glad) giver" — he delights in the willing heart, for alms are a sacrifice, and no sacrifice pleases God unless voluntary. The verse displays that giving should be willing and cheerful.

The witnesses agree: giving should follow a deliberate "purpose of the heart" (a settled resolve, not a passing impulse), each giving from a willing heart (Barnes, Ellicott); "not grudgingly (of grief, as sorry to part with it) nor of necessity (by constraint)," both reluctance and compulsion spoiling the gift (Barnes, Calvin, Clarke); for "God loveth a cheerful (hilaron) giver," delighting in the glad heart, since alms are a sacrifice and none pleases God unless voluntary (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays cheerful giving. The verse marks the purpose: of the heart. The verse displays the faults: grudging, constraint. The verse displays the motive: God loves the cheerful. The verse displays the principle: alms a voluntary sacrifice. The giving displays the willing, glad heart God loves. The verse displays the purpose: deliberate. The verse displays the cheerfulness: required. The verse displays the love: of God. That giving should be willing and cheerful, not grudging, displays the heart of true liberality: each is to give from a deliberate purpose of heart, neither reluctantly nor under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver — delighting not in the sum but in the glad and willing heart that offers it. The verse displays the cheerful giving God loves, enabled by his abounding grace (v.8).
8:
"And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work:" The brief verse notes God's power to supply all sufficiency for every good work. The grammatical voices note the promise. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's encouragement to the giver: "God is able to make all grace abound toward you" — to supply, overflowingly, every needed grace and good thing — so that they need not fear that giving will leave them in want.

On "always having all sufficiency in all things," the witnesses note the provision. Barnes and Calvin note the comprehensive promise: "always" (at all times), "all sufficiency" (autarkeia, enough, contentment, competence), "in all things" — God supplies the giver fully, that he be never in lack.

On "may abound to every good work," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes notes the end: that, so supplied, they may "abound to every good work" — their sufficiency overflowing in further generosity; God supplies that we may give the more. The verse displays God's power to supply all sufficiency for every good work.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "God is able to make all grace abound toward you," supplying overflowingly every needed grace, so giving need not leave them in want (Barnes, Robertson); the comprehensive promise — "always," "all sufficiency (competence, contentment)," "in all things," God supplying the giver fully (Barnes, Calvin); that they "may abound to every good work," their sufficiency overflowing in further generosity (Barnes). The verse displays God's abounding provision. The verse marks the power: God able. The verse displays the grace: made to abound. The verse displays the sufficiency: in all things. The verse displays the purpose: every good work. The promise displays God supplying the giver to give the more. The verse displays the grace: abounding. The verse displays the sufficiency: full. The verse displays the good work: abounding. God's power to supply all sufficiency for every good work displays the great encouragement to the giver: God is able to make all grace overflow toward them, so that, always having a full competence in all things, they may abound the more in every good work — he supplies that they may give. The verse displays the abounding provision of God, confirmed by the scattering giver (v.9).
9:
"(As it is written, He hath dispersed abroad; he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever." The brief verse notes the scriptural picture of the liberal man whose righteousness endures. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Psalm 112:9 (of the righteous, God-fearing man): "he hath dispersed abroad (scattered freely, as a sower); he hath given to the poor" — a picture of open-handed, generous giving.

On "his righteousness remaineth for ever," the witnesses note the permanence. Barnes and Calvin note that the liberal man's "righteousness (his beneficence, his right conduct, here especially his charity) remaineth for ever" — it is not lost but abides, in its fruit and reward, perpetually.

The Reformed Calvin notes the meaning: "righteousness" here includes the works of mercy; the generous man's good deeds are not transient but endure before God, who remembers and rewards them. The verse displays the scriptural picture of the liberal man whose righteousness endures.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Psalm 112:9 (of the righteous man) — "he hath dispersed abroad (scattered freely); he hath given to the poor," a picture of open-handed giving (Barnes, Robertson); and "his righteousness remaineth for ever," his beneficence not lost but abiding in its fruit and reward (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes "righteousness" here includes the works of mercy, enduring before God. The verse displays the liberal man's enduring righteousness. The verse marks the citation: Psalm 112. The verse displays the scattering: to the poor. The verse displays the permanence: righteousness for ever. The verse displays the meaning: works of mercy. The picture displays the generous man's charity abiding for ever. The verse displays the dispersing: abroad. The verse displays the giving: to the poor. The verse displays the righteousness: enduring. The scriptural picture of the liberal man whose righteousness endures displays the lasting worth of charity: as the psalm says of the righteous man who scatters freely and gives to the poor, his righteousness — his works of mercy — remains for ever, not lost but abiding in its fruit and reward before God. The verse displays the permanence of beneficence, sustained by God's continual supply (v.10).
10:
"Now he that ministereth seed to the sower both minister bread for your food, and multiply your seed sown, and increase the fruits of your righteousness;)" The brief verse notes God supplying and multiplying the giver's seed and fruit. The grammatical voices note the agriculture figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul prays (or affirms) that God, "he that ministereth (supplieth) seed to the sower and bread for food" (Isaiah 55:10), will "both minister bread for your food" (supply the giver's own needs) "and multiply your seed sown" (increase his means of giving).

On "increase the fruits of your righteousness," the witnesses note the harvest. Barnes and Chrysostom note God will "increase the fruits of your righteousness" — the harvest of their charity, both in its further effects and its reward; he multiplies both the seed (means) and the fruit (result) of their giving.

The Reformed Calvin notes the assurance: as God supplies the natural sower with seed and bread, so he will supply and multiply the spiritual sower's means and reward; the giver loses nothing but gains increase. The verse displays God supplying and multiplying the giver's seed and fruit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God, "he that ministereth seed to the sower and bread for food" (Isaiah 55:10), will "minister bread for your food" (supply the giver's needs) and "multiply your seed sown" (increase his means of giving, Barnes, Robertson); and "increase the fruits of your righteousness," the harvest of their charity in effect and reward (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes God supplies and multiplies the spiritual sower's means and reward. The verse displays God multiplying the giver's seed. The verse marks the supply: seed and bread. The verse displays the multiplying: of the seed. The verse displays the increase: of the fruit. The verse displays the assurance: the giver gains. The supply displays God increasing both the means and reward of giving. The verse displays the seed: ministered. The verse displays the bread: supplied. The verse displays the fruit: increased. God supplying and multiplying the giver's seed and fruit displays the divine guarantee behind liberality: as God gives the sower both seed and bread, so he supplies the giver's own needs, multiplies his means of giving, and increases the harvest of his charity — the generous lose nothing but gain increase. The verse displays God's enriching of the giver, overflowing in thanksgiving (v.11).
11:
"Being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness, which causeth through us thanksgiving to God." The brief verse notes the giver enriched for bountifulness that yields thanksgiving to God. The grammatical voices note the enrichment. Barnes and Robertson note that the givers are "enriched in every thing to all bountifulness (liberality)" — God enriches them not for hoarding but for the purpose of generous giving; their abundance is for openhandedness.

On "which causeth through us thanksgiving to God," the witnesses note the result. Barnes and Calvin note that this liberality, "through us" (through Paul's administration of the gift), "causeth thanksgiving to God" — the relief of the poor saints leads them to thank God; giving produces gratitude and praise to him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the chain: God enriches the giver, the giver relieves the poor, and the poor thank God — so the whole flows back to God's glory. The verse displays the giver enriched for bountifulness that yields thanksgiving.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the givers are "enriched in every thing to all bountifulness," God enriching them not for hoarding but for generous giving (Barnes, Robertson); this liberality, "through us" (Paul's administration), "causeth thanksgiving to God," the relief of the poor leading them to thank him (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the chain: God enriches, the giver relieves, the poor thank God. The verse displays enrichment for bountifulness. The verse marks the enrichment: in every thing. The verse displays the purpose: all bountifulness. The verse displays the result: thanksgiving to God. The verse displays the chain: back to God's glory. The enrichment displays giving issuing in thanksgiving to God. The verse displays the riches: for giving. The verse displays the bountifulness: enabled. The verse displays the thanks: to God. The giver enriched for bountifulness that yields thanksgiving to God displays the upward end of liberality: God enriches the givers for generous giving, and their bounty, administered to the poor, causes thanksgiving to God — so the whole returns to his glory. The verse displays the thanksgiving produced by giving, abounding in the ministry of relief (v.12).
12:
"For the administration of this service not only supplieth the want of the saints, but is abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God;" The brief verse notes the collection both relieving want and abounding in thanksgiving. The grammatical voices note the double effect. Barnes and Robertson note that "the administration of this service (the collection, the ministering)" has a twofold result: it "not only supplieth the want of the saints" (relieves the material need of the poor believers at Jerusalem) but does more.

On "abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God," the witnesses note the greater fruit. Barnes and Calvin note the collection is "abundant (overflowing) also by many thanksgivings unto God" — it overflows in the gratitude and praise that the relieved saints render to God; the spiritual fruit exceeds the material relief.

The Reformed Calvin notes the worth: the giving is doubly valuable — it meets bodily need and it multiplies praise to God; the highest end of charity is the thanksgiving it brings forth. The verse displays the collection both relieving want and abounding in thanksgiving.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "the administration of this service (the collection)" has a twofold result — it "not only supplieth the want of the saints" (relieves the poor) (Barnes, Robertson); but is "abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God," overflowing in the gratitude the relieved saints render to God, the spiritual fruit exceeding the material relief (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the giving is doubly valuable. The verse displays the double fruit of the collection. The verse marks the service: the collection. The verse displays the relief: of the saints' want. The verse displays the abundance: of thanksgivings. The verse displays the worth: doubly valuable. The administration displays the gift relieving want and multiplying praise. The verse displays the supply: of want. The verse displays the thanksgiving: many. The verse displays the fruit: to God. The collection both relieving want and abounding in thanksgiving displays the twofold worth of Christian charity: the ministering not only meets the bodily need of the poor saints but overflows in the many thanksgivings they render to God — its spiritual fruit surpassing even its material relief. The verse displays the praise produced by giving, glorifying God through their obedience (v.13).
13:
"Whiles by the experiment of this ministration they glorify God for your professed subjection unto the gospel of Christ, and for your liberal distribution unto them, and unto all men;" The brief verse notes the saints glorifying God for the Corinthians' obedience and liberality. The grammatical voices note the testimony. Barnes and Robertson note that "by the experiment (the proof, the tested evidence) of this ministration (the collection)," the relieved saints "glorify God" — the gift was a demonstration that moved them to praise God for the givers.

On "your professed subjection unto the gospel... and your liberal distribution," the witnesses note the two grounds. Barnes and Calvin note they glorify God for two things: the Corinthians' "professed subjection (obedience, confession) unto the gospel of Christ" (their genuine faith, proved by deeds) and their "liberal distribution (generous sharing) unto them and unto all men."

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the proof: the liberality was visible evidence that the Corinthians' profession of the gospel was real; their giving authenticated their faith and brought glory to God. The verse displays the saints glorifying God for the Corinthians' obedience and liberality.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "by the experiment (the proof) of this ministration," the relieved saints "glorify God," the gift a demonstration moving them to praise (Barnes, Robertson); on two grounds — the Corinthians' "professed subjection unto the gospel" (genuine faith proved by deeds) and their "liberal distribution unto them and unto all men" (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the liberality authenticated their faith. The verse displays glorifying God for their obedience. The verse marks the proof: of the ministration. The verse displays the glory: to God. The verse displays the grounds: obedience and liberality. The verse displays the proof: faith authenticated. The testimony displays the gift proving faith and bringing glory to God. The verse displays the subjection: to the gospel. The verse displays the distribution: liberal. The verse displays the glory: to God. The saints glorifying God for the Corinthians' obedience and liberality displays the witness borne by the gift: the tested proof of the collection moved the relieved saints to praise God both for the Corinthians' genuine obedience to the gospel and for their generous sharing — their giving authenticating their faith. The verse displays the glory brought to God by liberality, drawing forth the saints' prayers (v.14).
14:
"And by their prayer for you, which long after you for the exceeding grace of God in you." The brief verse notes the saints' prayer and longing love for the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the response. Barnes and Robertson note the further fruit of the gift: the relieved saints respond "by their prayer for you" — they pray for the Corinthians — and "long after you" (yearn for you with affection); the gift binds the churches in love.

On "for the exceeding grace of God in you," the witnesses note the cause. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of their love and prayer: "the exceeding (surpassing) grace of God in you" — the abundant grace seen in the Corinthians' liberality and faith drew out the saints' affection and intercession.

The Reformed Calvin notes the bond: the gift created a holy reciprocity — the Corinthians gave material aid, and the Jerusalem saints returned love and prayer; the communion of saints was strengthened. The verse displays the saints' prayer and longing love for the Corinthians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the relieved saints respond "by their prayer for you" and "long after you" (yearn for you with affection), the gift binding the churches in love (Barnes, Robertson); the ground being "the exceeding grace of God in you," the abundant grace in the Corinthians' liberality drawing out the saints' affection (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the gift created a holy reciprocity. The verse displays the saints' prayer and love. The verse marks the prayer: for the Corinthians. The verse displays the longing: after them. The verse displays the cause: the grace in them. The verse displays the bond: reciprocal love. The response displays the gift binding the churches in love and prayer. The verse displays the prayer: of the saints. The verse displays the longing: their love. The verse displays the grace: exceeding. The saints' prayer and longing love for the Corinthians display the holy reciprocity the gift created: the relieved believers pray for and yearn after the Corinthians, drawn by the exceeding grace of God seen in their liberality — material aid returned in love and intercession. The verse displays the communion of saints strengthened by giving, crowned with thanks for God's gift (v.15).
15:
"Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift." The brief verse notes the doxology of thanks for God's unspeakable gift. The grammatical voices note the climax. Barnes and Robertson note Paul closes the whole discourse on giving with a sudden burst of praise: "thanks be unto God for his unspeakable (indescribable, beyond words) gift" — the contemplation of grace overflows into thanksgiving.

On "his unspeakable gift," the witnesses note the gift meant. Barnes and Wesley note that the "unspeakable gift" is, most fully, Christ himself — God's supreme, indescribable gift (John 3:16) — though some include the whole grace of the gospel and the fruits of liberality just described; the gift that surpasses all words.

The grammatical-historical note the fitness: having traced the chain of grace and thanksgiving in giving, Paul lifts his eyes to the fountain — God's own unspeakable gift of his Son — the pattern and source of all Christian generosity. The verse displays the doxology of thanks for God's unspeakable gift.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul closes the discourse on giving with a burst of praise — "thanks be unto God for his unspeakable (indescribable, beyond words) gift," the contemplation of grace overflowing into thanksgiving (Barnes, Robertson); the "unspeakable gift" being most fully Christ himself, God's supreme gift (John 3:16), though some include the whole grace described (Barnes, Wesley). The grammatical-historical note Paul lifts his eyes to the fountain of all generosity. The verse displays the doxology of thanks. The verse marks the praise: thanks to God. The verse displays the gift: unspeakable. The verse displays the meaning: Christ himself. The verse displays the fitness: the source of giving. The doxology displays Paul praising God for his indescribable gift. The verse displays the thanks: to God. The verse displays the gift: beyond words. The verse displays the climax: of the discourse. The doxology of thanks for God's unspeakable gift displays the crown of the whole appeal: having traced the grace and thanksgiving that flow from giving, Paul bursts into praise for God's own indescribable gift — Christ himself — the supreme pattern and fountain of all Christian generosity. The verse displays the thanksgiving that closes the discourse on giving, before Paul turns to defend his ministry (ch. 10).
